sections.heading: Overview: Cal/OSHA Heat Illness Prevention Standard

content.text: California's Heat Illness Prevention standard (8 CCR §3395) is one of the most comprehensive in the nation and is mandatory for all outdoor and high-heat indoor work environments. For Contoso Retail California warehouses — particularly those with limited climate control, receiving docks, or outdoor loading areas — compliance with §3395 is required.

Heat illness includes heat cramps, heat exhaustion, heat syncope (fainting), and the life-threatening heat stroke. Contoso Retail takes a zero-tolerance approach to skipping heat illness prevention procedures.

Every California facility must have a written Heat Illness Prevention Plan. The plan is site-specific and must be available to all employees and to Cal/OSHA upon request. Key required elements include:

items: Procedures for providing water (at least 1 quart/hour per employee).
Procedures for providing shade and cool-down rest periods.
Acclimatization procedures for new and returning workers.
High-heat procedures (temperatures at or above 95°F).
Emergency response procedures for suspected heat illness.
Employee and supervisor training requirements.

content.text: The Warehouse Manager maintains the written plan and reviews it annually before summer. The plan is posted in the break room and available digitally on the Safety Coordinator's shared drive.
Cal/OSHA §3395 requires employers to provide potable drinking water to employees working in heat conditions. For Contoso Retail California facilities:

items: A minimum of 1 quart (32 oz) of cool, potable water per employee per hour must be available.
Water stations must be located as close as practicable to the work area — not only in the break room.
Water coolers are stocked and maintained by the supervisor at the start of each shift. Refill is the supervisor's responsibility.
Personal water bottles are permitted and encouraged; the facility provides additional cups at water stations.
Electrolyte supplements may be provided during extreme heat conditions — check with the Safety Coordinator.

content.text: Shade is mandatory under Cal/OSHA §3395 when the temperature reaches or exceeds 80°F in the work area. For Contoso Retail California warehouse operations:

items: When outdoor temperature exceeds 80°F, shaded rest areas must be made available within reasonable proximity to the work area.
Shade areas must accommodate the number of workers on break at one time.
Cool-down rest: Any employee who feels overheated has the right to take a preventive cool-down rest in the shade for at least 5 minutes. This is not chargeable as a regular break.
Supervisors must not discourage or prevent employees from taking preventive cool-down rests.
If an employee shows signs of heat illness during a cool-down rest, treat as a potential medical emergency — monitor the employee for at least 15 minutes before returning to work.

content.text: Acclimatization — the process of gradually adapting to heat — is required for new workers and for workers returning after 10+ days away. Studies show that most heat-related deaths occur in the first few days of working in heat.

items: New workers and returning workers must not be assigned to full workload in hot conditions immediately.
Day 1–3: Assign to no more than 60% of normal workload in the hottest conditions.
Day 4–5: Increase to 80% of normal workload.
Day 6+: Full workload permitted, with continued monitoring.
Supervisors must check on newly acclimatizing workers more frequently during the acclimatization period.
Acclimatization status is tracked on the shift staffing sheet during heat season (May–October).

content.text: When outdoor temperature reaches or is predicted to reach 95°F, Cal/OSHA §3395 requires enhanced high-heat procedures. These apply to all outdoor work areas and any indoor work area where temperatures may approach or exceed 95°F (e.g., receiving docks during summer):

items: Supervisor or designee must observe workers for signs of heat illness at least every hour.
Workers must be reminded to drink water frequently (every 15–20 minutes).
Mandatory pre-shift briefing on heat illness prevention and emergency response.
Ensure communication devices (radio, phone) are functional for emergency contact.
Identify the name of the employee designated to contact emergency services if heat illness occurs.
Consider schedule modifications (shift earlier start, extended rest periods, rotation of hot tasks).

sections.heading: Signs of Heat Illness & Emergency Response

content.text: All employees and supervisors must be able to recognize the signs of heat illness and know how to respond:

rows: Muscle pain or spasms, often in abdomen, arms, or legs
Move to cool area; drink water or sports drink; rest; do not return to hot work for a few hours
Heavy sweating, weakness, dizziness, nausea, headache, pale cool clammy skin
Move to cool area; loosen clothing; apply cool wet cloths; drink cool water; if not improved in 15 min — call 911
High body temp (103°F+), hot red dry or damp skin, rapid pulse, confusion, unconsciousness
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY. Cool the person by any means (ice packs, cool water immersion, wet towels). This is life-threatening.

content.text: Never leave a worker with suspected heat stroke alone. Stay with them until emergency services arrive. Do not give fluids to an unconscious person.
